articles.title: Pentagon releases initial batch of declassified files detailing UFOs
articles.summary: The Pentagon released a batch of “never‑before‑seen” declassified files on UFOs after President Donald Trump directed the agency to do so earlier in the year. The files contain investigations of reported sightings that span decades, including a large FBI file with hundreds of pages of eyewitness testimonies and public reports from 1947 to 1968 and more recent military memos describing a possible small UAP in Iraq in 2022 and multiple glares or lights of unknown origin observed in Syria in 2024. The Pentagon said the documents are now available to the public with no clearance required and will post new materials on a rolling basis every few weeks. A disclaimer on the Pentagon website notes that the descriptive language in the military memos reflects the subjective interpretation of the writer and should not be taken as a conclusive indication of what actually happened. The files also include reports from Apollo 12 astronauts in 1969 who saw flashes of light sailing off the Moon and from Apollo 17 astronauts in 1972 who saw bright particles of light that they theorized might be chunks of ice. Trump’s Truth Social post and NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman’s statement both praised the release as a step toward greater transparency about unidentified anomalous phenomena.

articles.title: A UFO Whistleblower died of an "accidental overdose" months before he was set to testify to congress and now the FBI is now investigating
articles.summary: Matthew James Sullivan, a 39‑year‑old Air Force veteran who had worked for the Air Force Intelligence Agency, the National Air and Space Intelligence Center and the NSA, was found dead in his Falls Church, Virginia home on May 12 2024; the medical examiner ruled his death an accidental overdose of alcohol, alprazolam, cyclobenzaprine and imipramine. Sullivan had been scheduled to testify before Congress in November 2024 about the government’s secret UFO crash‑retrieval program and had personally seen UFOs in federal possession. Representative Eric Burlison sent a letter to FBI Director Kash Patel expressing concern that Sullivan’s sudden death raised “substantial questions” and potential foul play, citing national‑security implications. The FBI responded that it is “spearheading the effort to look for connections into the missing and deceased scientists” and is working with the Department of Energy, the Department of War, and state and local law‑enforcement partners. Retired Major General David Abba, who directed the Pentagon’s special access programs, said at Sullivan’s funeral that Sullivan “held the burden that a select few in this nation have of truly understanding what’s going on.” The article notes a pattern of other whistleblowers—such as David Grusch, David Wilcock and Amy Eskridge—who have died under circumstances the authorities have ruled accidental or self‑inflicted.

articles.title: Multiple Pastors Claim U.S. Military Intelligence Officers Secretly Briefed Them To Prepare Their Congregations For UAP Disclosure
articles.summary: Several religious leaders, including Pastor Perry Stone and evangelist Tony Merkel, claim they were privately briefed by U.S. military intelligence officers to prepare their congregations for an upcoming UAP disclosure. Stone, a Tennessee‑based Pentecostal evangelist, said in a video that a trusted associate informed him that a group of pastors was invited to hear directly from government‑connected individuals about an imminent disclosure event involving unidentified anomalous phenomena. Stone described the briefing as covering craft not allegedly part of our planet, non‑human beings, and a coordinated propaganda plan already in motion. Merkel says he has been in contact with officials for over a year, describing them as Christians in intelligence operations assigned to work within the UAP disclosure community, and that they approached him with a mission to prepare the body of Christ for what is coming. The state where the briefing took place has not been disclosed, and no government memo, official invitation, transcript, or attendee list has surfaced. The White House has not denied the claims, and Washington has remained silent, a silence the article notes is significant.

articles.title: Was Matthew James Sullivan Married? Inside The UFO Whistleblower's Life Amid His 'Suspicious' Death
articles.summary: Matthew James Sullivan, a 39‑year‑old Air Force veteran known by the call sign “Quake,” was found dead at his home in 2024 from an accidental overdose. Sullivan had served in the U.S. Air Force Intelligence, worked for the National Air and Space Intelligence Centre, the Air Force Intelligence Agency, and the National Security Agency, and received a Bronze Star for valor during Operation Enduring Freedom. His death has been discussed alongside the recent deaths of UFO researchers David Wilcock and Nick Pope, all of whom were linked to sensitive areas such as nuclear research, advanced aerospace, and UFO/UAP studies. Reports from the New York Post claim that 11 to 14 scientists and experts connected to aerospace, defense, or advanced research have died or gone missing in recent years, prompting federal attention. FBI officials, with the help of multiple agencies, are reviewing all reported cases to determine whether the incidents are coincidental or part of a larger pattern. At Sullivan’s funeral, retired Major General David Abba remarked that Sullivan carried “the burden that a select few in this nation have of truly understanding what’s going on,” fueling speculation that Sullivan’s death may not have been a mere accident, though no official confirmation supports this claim.

articles.title: ‘SLEEPING DOG’ Trailer Dives Into Jeremy Corbell’s Fight For UAP Disclosure
articles.summary: The trailer for “SLEEPING DOG” offers a first look at a documentary that follows investigative journalist and filmmaker Jeremy Corbell and his controversial rise in the UAP disclosure movement. Directed by Michael Lazovsky, the film has been described as a real‑life “Disclosure Day” and charts Corbell’s transition from mixed martial artist to a prominent figure pushing for transparency around unidentified aerial phenomena. It will open in select theaters on May 8, 2026, before becoming available on digital platforms on May 12. The documentary explores Corbell’s long partnership with investigative reporter George Knapp and their attempts to bring military‑filmed UAP footage and whistleblower testimony to the public eye. It promises unprecedented access and never‑before‑seen footage from Corbell’s earliest investigations, featuring figures such as astronaut Edgar Mitchell, John Lear, Bob Lazar, David Grusch, David Fravor, John B. Alexander and others involved in the UAP transparency movement. The film is produced by Jeremy Corbell, Jim Martin and Michael Lazovsky and aims to provide an intimate, high‑stakes look at one of the most polarizing subjects of the modern era.

articles.title: UAP Whistleblower David Grusch on “Non-human” Biologics & Craft
articles.summary: David Grusch served 14 years in the Air Force, reaching the rank of Major and completing a combat tour in Afghanistan. He held senior intelligence positions at the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency and the National Reconnaissance Office. Grusch represented the NRO on the Unidentified Aerial Phenomena Task Force from 2019 to 2021. In 2022 he filed a whistleblower complaint that was described as credible and urgent. In 2023 he testified before Congress. He claimed that elements of the U.S. government had thwarted Congressional oversight, withheld information from proper Executive Branch officials, and illegally concealed programs related to the recovery and reverse engineering of craft of unknown, non‑human origin.

articles.title: Rep. Anna Paulina Luna Frames UFO Disclosure Through The Book of Enoch As A Window Into A Reality Beyond Human Perception
articles.summary: Rep. Anna Paulina Luna of Florida publicly suggested that the ongoing discussion about unidentified aerial phenomena may be closely connected to themes found in the Book of Enoch. She frames this link through the interdimensional hypothesis and Carl Sagan’s Flatland teaching model, arguing that both the modern UAP conversation and the ancient text describe interactions between different layers of existence. The UAP conversation, acknowledged by NASA and the Department of Defense, includes documented observations of sudden acceleration, seamless trans‑medium travel, and behavior that does not align with known propulsion systems or aerodynamics. Luna’s remarks emphasize that the Book of Enoch is not presented as scientific evidence but as an early attempt to describe encounters that lacked a clear vocabulary, mirroring the structural parallels of the interdimensional hypothesis. She does not claim that UAPs are definitively linked to the Watchers of the Book of Enoch, noting that no verified evidence currently supports such a connection. The article warns that introducing religious or historical texts into a policy‑driven discussion carries risks of misinterpretation and speculation, while acknowledging that the disclosure process is revealing complexity rather than simple answers.

articles.title: The Rendlesham Forest mystery: ‘It’s the perfect storm of a UFO case’
articles.summary: Nick Pope, who worked for the UK Ministry of Defence from 1985 to 2006 and headed the UFO desk from 1991 to 1994, said in a 1996 interview that after reviewing evidence he believed that 5–10 % of UFO sightings could not be explained by conventional means and might be extraterrestrial craft. Pope’s research led him to focus on the Rendlesham Forest incident of 24–26 December 1980, when two American airmen reported strange blinking red, blue and white lights near RAF Bentwaters, a US base that housed nuclear missiles. The case is described by Pope as a “perfect storm” because it involved multiple military witnesses, radar confirmation, physical evidence such as ground indentations and scorch marks, and declassified documents now available at the National Archives and the MoD website. Pope co‑authored the 2014 book Encounter in Rendlesham Forest with eyewitnesses Jim Penniston and John Burroughs, who described how they investigated the lights, found a triangular craft with no landing gear, and observed a bright, static‑like burst of light that left them stunned. Penniston reported that the craft was about six and a half feet tall, had a dorsal fin and engravings resembling Egyptian hieroglyphs, and that its surface felt warm and smooth, while Burroughs was frozen in a beam of white/blue light. The incident remains unexplained, with the witnesses noting that the craft did not move when they attempted to push it and that the surrounding area showed unusual indentations, suggesting technology beyond current human capability.

articles.title: Conspiracy theory over UFOs and missing scientists spreads from web to White House
articles.summary: A conspiracy theory claiming that the disappearances or deaths of at least 11 U.S. scientists linked to space, defense, and nuclear research are part of a plot involving China or other state enemies and UFOs has spread from the internet to right‑wing media, mainstream press, and prompted congressional inquiry and questions from Donald Trump. The theory began with the disappearance of retired Air Force Major General William “Neil” McCasland on 27 February, who left his home in Albuquerque with his phone and glasses behind, was reported missing by his wife, and a silver alert was issued, but no trace of him has been found. Other cases cited in the theory include NASA scientist Michael David Hicks, who died in 2023; Monica Reza, who vanished while hiking in 2023; astrophysicist Carl Grillmair, who was shot on his porch; MIT physicist Nuno Loureiro, who was killed by a former classmate; chemical biologist Jason Thomas, whose remains were found in Massachusetts; and researcher Amy Eskridge, who died by suicide in 2022. Republican lawmakers James Comer and Eric Burlison sent a letter to the FBI, Department of Energy, NASA, and other agencies demanding an investigation into a “possible sinister connection” among the disappearances, citing the deaths of two Los Alamos National Laboratory workers and the alleged close professional link between Reza and McCasland. Investigators have said they are investigating every possible link, but have not confirmed any connection, and the White House has not confirmed any link between the cases. Experts such as Greg Eghigian and Susan McCasland Wilkerson have argued that the narrative is driven by a convergence of existing fears about UFOs, nuclear facilities, and state secrets, and that there is no evidence to support the claim that the scientists were abducted or targeted for their knowledge.

summaries.batch-declassified-detailing-files-initi: The Pentagon released a batch of “never‑before‑seen” declassified files on UFOs after President Donald Trump directed the agency to do so earlier in the year. The files contain investigations of reported sightings that span decades, including a large FBI file with hundreds of pages of eyewitness testimonies and public reports from 1947 to 1968 and more recent military memos describing a possible small UAP in Iraq in 2022 and multiple glares or lights of unknown origin observed in Syria in 2024. The Pentagon said the documents are now available to the public with no clearance required and will post new materials on a rolling basis every few weeks. A disclaimer on the Pentagon website notes that the descriptive language in the military memos reflects the subjective interpretation of the writer and should not be taken as a conclusive indication of what actually happened. The files also include reports from Apollo 12 astronauts in 1969 who saw flashes of light sailing off the Moon and from Apollo 17 astronauts in 1972 who saw bright particles of light that they theorized might be chunks of ice. Trump’s Truth Social post and NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman’s statement both praised the release as a step toward greater transparency about unidentified anomalous phenomena.
summaries.amid-anomalous-can-conclusions-disclosur: The Pentagon has begun releasing new files on UFOs, stating that the public can draw their own conclusions about unidentified anomalous phenomena. The release effort is coordinated by the White House, the director of national intelligence, the Energy Department, NASA, and the FBI. In a post on X, the Pentagon said that while past administrations sought to discredit or dissuade the public, President Donald Trump is focused on maximum transparency and that additional documents will be released on a rolling basis. Congress created an office in 2022 to declassify UFO material, and a 2024 report from that office found hundreds of new UAP incidents but no evidence that the U.S. government had confirmed alien technology. A small group of Republican members of Congress, including Rep. Anna Paulina Luna and Rep. Tim Burchett, have urged further transparency, with Luna demanding 46 UAP videos identified by whistleblowers and Burchett noting that transparency will take time. Experts have cautioned that UAP videos are often misinterpreted and mischaracterized by those unfamiliar with advanced military technology, and the Pentagon’s 2024 report rebutted claims that the U.S. government had recovered alien technology or confirmed evidence of alien life.
summaries.classified-files-previously-releases-tru: Under President Donald Trump’s direction, the Department of War, with support from the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, reviewed and declassified several UFO files for public release. The released files include 162 unresolved Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) records archived by the federal government. Trump announced the release on February 19 2026, noting “tremendous interest” by the layman. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth said the Department of War is in lockstep with Trump to provide unprecedented transparency regarding the government’s understanding of UAPs. Tulsi Gabbard, Director of National Intelligence, stated that the Office of the Director of National Intelligence is coordinating the Intelligence Community’s declassification efforts with the Department of War to ensure a careful, comprehensive review. FBI Director Kash Patel remarked that the FBI is proud to support this landmark release and will continue to prioritize national security as the files are reviewed and released.
summaries.accidental-congress-died-fbi-investigati: Matthew James Sullivan, a 39‑year‑old Air Force veteran who had worked for the Air Force Intelligence Agency, the National Air and Space Intelligence Center and the NSA, was found dead in his Falls Church, Virginia home on May 12 2024; the medical examiner ruled his death an accidental overdose of alcohol, alprazolam, cyclobenzaprine and imipramine. Sullivan had been scheduled to testify before Congress in November 2024 about the government’s secret UFO crash‑retrieval program and had personally seen UFOs in federal possession. Representative Eric Burlison sent a letter to FBI Director Kash Patel expressing concern that Sullivan’s sudden death raised “substantial questions” and potential foul play, citing national‑security implications. The FBI responded that it is “spearheading the effort to look for connections into the missing and deceased scientists” and is working with the Department of Energy, the Department of War, and state and local law‑enforcement partners. Retired Major General David Abba, who directed the Pentagon’s special access programs, said at Sullivan’s funeral that Sullivan “held the burden that a select few in this nation have of truly understanding what’s going on.” The article notes a pattern of other whistleblowers—such as David Grusch, David Wilcock and Amy Eskridge—who have died under circumstances the authorities have ruled accidental or self‑inflicted.
summaries.asked-being-files-government-learn-relea: On May 8, the Pentagon began posting declassified documents, photos, and videos related to unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP) after President Trump urged the release in February 2023. The release includes evidence such as a video of a U.S. missile striking an unidentified glowing orb and a government report noting more than 750 new UAP sightings between May 2023 and June 2024. Former AARO director Sean Kirkpatrick said the files contain no proof of extraterrestrial life, describing the Trump order as a distraction and noting that his office had to declassify only what could be released. NASA UAP panel member Federica Bianco and astrophysicist Neil de Grasse Tyson both stated that the documents are unlikely to reveal alien technology or phenomena that violate physics, and that most sightings can be explained by ignorance of optical or atmospheric effects. UCSD astrophysicist Shelley Wright expects most documents to be heavily redacted for national‑security reasons but is hopeful that declassified older data could be studied with modern technology, while Barnard professor Janna Levin says astronomers are excited about the possibility of finding microbial evidence rather than advanced alien tech. Overall, scientists anticipate that the release will provide data for analysis but are not expecting definitive evidence of extraterrestrial life, and they emphasize the need for skepticism and rigorous scientific inquiry.
summaries.briefed-claim-congregations-disclosure-i: Several religious leaders, including Pastor Perry Stone and evangelist Tony Merkel, claim they were privately briefed by U.S. military intelligence officers to prepare their congregations for an upcoming UAP disclosure. Stone, a Tennessee‑based Pentecostal evangelist, said in a video that a trusted associate informed him that a group of pastors was invited to hear directly from government‑connected individuals about an imminent disclosure event involving unidentified anomalous phenomena. Stone described the briefing as covering craft not allegedly part of our planet, non‑human beings, and a coordinated propaganda plan already in motion. Merkel says he has been in contact with officials for over a year, describing them as Christians in intelligence operations assigned to work within the UAP disclosure community, and that they approached him with a mission to prepare the body of Christ for what is coming. The state where the briefing took place has not been disclosed, and no government memo, official invitation, transcript, or attendee list has surfaced. The White House has not denied the claims, and Washington has remained silent, a silence the article notes is significant.

Among the releases are NASA Apollo 12 and Apollo 17 photographs of lunar sky anomalies, a Pentagon review suggesting a physical object may explain one Apollo 17 image, and a report of an Apollo 17 astronaut seeing a flash north of Grimaldi crater. The videos, totaling 41 minutes, show infrared footage of white objects moving at high speeds, including a Greek 2023 clip of an object making 90‑degree turns at about 80 miles per hour, and other clips from the Indo‑Pacific, Syria and the U.S. that depict semi‑transparent orange orbs and a football‑shaped object. The Pentagon noted that 108 of the 162 files contain redactions to protect eyewitness identities, facility locations, or sensitive military information, but no redactions were made to information about the nature or existence of UAP encounters.

summaries.corbell-disclosure-dives-dog-fight-jerem: The trailer for “SLEEPING DOG” offers a first look at a documentary that follows investigative journalist and filmmaker Jeremy Corbell and his controversial rise in the UAP disclosure movement. Directed by Michael Lazovsky, the film has been described as a real‑life “Disclosure Day” and charts Corbell’s transition from mixed martial artist to a prominent figure pushing for transparency around unidentified aerial phenomena. summaries.biologics-craft-david-grusch-human-non-w: David Grusch served 14 years in the Air Force, reaching the rank of Major and completing a combat tour in Afghanistan. He claimed that elements of the U.S. government had thwarted Congressional oversight, withheld information from proper Executive Branch officials, and illegally concealed programs related to the recovery and reverse engineering of craft of unknown, non‑human origin.
summaries.anna-beyond-book-disclosure-enoch-frames: Rep. She frames this link through the interdimensional hypothesis and Carl Sagan’s Flatland teaching model, arguing that both the modern UAP conversation and the ancient text describe interactions between different layers of existence. The UAP conversation, acknowledged by NASA and the Department of Defense, includes documented observations of sudden acceleration, seamless trans‑medium travel, and behavior that does not align with known propulsion systems or aerodynamics. In February, Trump directed federal agencies to release records on extraterrestrial life, unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP), and UFOs, and he has since teased an imminent release of documents that have never before been shared. The Pentagon has been declassifying UFO documents for years, and a 2024 report from the All‑Domain Anomaly Resolution Office identified hundreds of new UAP incidents but found no evidence that the U.S. government had confirmed alien technology. Former director Sean Kirkpatrick said that Trump’s promises are bluster and that there are no bombshell revelations, noting that the government’s records contain no photos or interviews with aliens. A GOP‑led Task Force on the Declassification of Federal Secrets, co‑chaired by Florida Representative Anna Paulina Luna, has demanded UAP videos from the Pentagon, but a deadline set by Luna passed without any videos being produced. Vice President JD Vance has described himself as “obsessed” with UFO files and believes that sightings are the work of spiritual demons, while Trump himself has expressed uncertainty about the existence of extraterrestrial life.

summaries.administration-america-file-gov-historic: On May 8, 2026, the U.S. Department of War announced the first release of previously classified government files on Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) through a new website, war.gov/ufo, as part of the Presidential Unsealing and Reporting System for UAP Encounters (PURSUE). PURSUE is an interagency effort that includes the White House, the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, the Department of Energy, AARO, NASA, the FBI, and other intelligence community components. The initial release contains more than 160 documents, including State Department and FBI reports, NASA mission transcripts, eyewitness accounts, photographs, a 1947 Pan Am crew sighting, and a 2023 drone pilot report. The Department of War states that the archived materials involve unresolved cases, meaning the government has not made a definitive determination about the nature of the observed phenomena, and that unresolved status may result from limited data, incomplete information, or the need for additional technical analysis. The release does not confirm extraterrestrial life, alien technology, or recovered alien materials; it simply acknowledges that some UAP cases remain unresolved and that the public has a right to view the underlying records. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth framed the release as a transparency effort aligned with President Donald J. Trump's order, and the government plans to continue rolling out additional files on a regular basis.
summaries.but-files-green-hints-lots-men-released: The Department of Defense began the initial release of government files on Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP) on Friday, fulfilling an order from President Trump to disclose documents related to aliens and UFOs. One file contains eyewitness accounts from federal employees who observed orbs in the dusk sky on two separate days in the Western United States. The release also features images capturing anomalies from the Apollo 17 and Apollo 12 moon missions. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the files have long fueled speculation and that it is time the American people see them. The DoD noted that all files have been reviewed for security, but many have not yet been analyzed to resolve the anomalies.

Tim Burchett, a member of the House Task Force on Declassification of Federal Secrets, warned that classified briefings on unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP) contain information so profound it would shock the American public and cause widespread concern. In a Newsmax interview, Burchett said he had been briefed by almost every alphabet agency and that full release of the material would “unglue” the country and force citizens to demand answers. He referenced a classified session held about two weeks earlier, describing the information as something that “would have set the Earth on fire,” but did not disclose details, citing legal restrictions on handling sensitive information. Burchett urged President Donald Trump, who earlier this year pledged to direct agencies to release files on UFOs, UAP, and extraterrestrial life, to push for full disclosure, while noting that he is not suicidal and does not take risks amid allegations of threats against those with UAP knowledge. Matt Gaetz’s unverified claims of secret military hybrid breeding programs involving extraterrestrials, and the unexplained disappearance of retired Air Force Maj. Gen. William “Neil” McCasland, a former Air Force Research Laboratory commander, which has drawn attention due to his background in advanced aerospace research. Skeptics, including former Rep. summaries.case-forest-mystery-perfect-rendlesham-s: Nick Pope, who worked for the UK Ministry of Defence from 1985 to 2006 and headed the UFO desk from 1991 to 1994, said in a 1996 interview that after reviewing evidence he believed that 5–10 % of UFO sightings could not be explained by conventional means and might be extraterrestrial craft. Pope’s research led him to focus on the Rendlesham Forest incident of 24–26 December 1980, when two American airmen reported strange blinking red, blue and white lights near RAF Bentwaters, a US base that housed nuclear missiles. The case is described by Pope as a “perfect storm” because it involved multiple military witnesses, radar confirmation, physical evidence such as ground indentations and scorch marks, and declassified documents now available at the National Archives and the MoD website. Pope co‑authored the 2014 book Encounter in Rendlesham Forest with eyewitnesses Jim Penniston and John Burroughs, who described how they investigated the lights, found a triangular craft with no landing gear, and observed a bright, static‑like burst of light that left them stunned. Penniston reported that the craft was about six and a half feet tall, had a dorsal fin and engravings resembling Egyptian hieroglyphs, and that its surface felt warm and smooth, while Burroughs was frozen in a beam of white/blue light. The incident remains unexplained, with the witnesses noting that the craft did not move when they attempted to push it and that the surrounding area showed unusual indentations, suggesting technology beyond current human capability.

summaries.conspiracy-house-missing-scientists-spre: A conspiracy theory claiming that the disappearances or deaths of at least 11 U.S. scientists linked to space, defense, and nuclear research are part of a plot involving China or other state enemies and UFOs has spread from the internet to right‑wing media, mainstream press, and prompted congressional inquiry and questions from Donald Trump. The theory began with the disappearance of retired Air Force Major General William “Neil” McCasland on 27 February, who left his home in Albuquerque with his phone and glasses behind, was reported missing by his wife, and a silver alert was issued, but no trace of him has been found. Other cases cited in the theory include NASA scientist Michael David Hicks, who died in 2023; Monica Reza, who vanished while hiking in 2023; astrophysicist Carl Grillmair, who was shot on his porch; MIT physicist Nuno Loureiro, who was killed by a former classmate; chemical biologist Jason Thomas, whose remains were found in Massachusetts; and researcher Amy Eskridge, who died by suicide in 2022. Republican lawmakers James Comer and Eric Burlison sent a letter to the FBI, Department of Energy, NASA, and other agencies demanding an investigation into a “possible sinister connection” among the disappearances, citing the deaths of two Los Alamos National Laboratory workers and the alleged close professional link between Reza and McCasland. Investigators have said they are investigating every possible link, but have not confirmed any connection, and the White House has not confirmed any link between the cases. Experts such as Greg Eghigian and Susan McCasland Wilkerson have argued that the narrative is driven by a convergence of existing fears about UFOs, nuclear facilities, and state secrets, and that there is no evidence to support the claim that the scientists were abducted or targeted for their knowledge.
summaries.encounters-presidential-pursue-reporting: The Presidential Unsealing and Reporting System for UAP Encounters (PURSUE) was created in response to a directive from President Donald J. Trump to increase transparency about unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) and related government records. The Department of War, with support from the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, is coordinating a nationwide effort to locate, review, declassify, and publicly release unresolved UAP‑related documents held by federal agencies. The initiative involves dozens of agencies and the examination of tens of millions of records, many of which exist only on paper and span several decades. The Department of War will publish new materials on a rolling basis, releasing tranches of declassified documents every few weeks as they are discovered. The released files are classified as unresolved cases, meaning the government cannot definitively determine the nature of the observed phenomena due to insufficient data. The Department of War also plans to continue separate reporting on resolved UAP cases as required by statute. Release 01 of the PURSUE archive was cleared for release on May 8, 2026, and includes a statement from Secretary of War Pete Hegseth affirming the administration’s commitment to unprecedented transparency.
summaries.2026-early-files-images-never-releases-s: On 8 May 2026 the US Department of War released the first batch of secret files documenting reported sightings of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) dating back to the 1940s.
